topic: Traits - Derive Macros for Custom Traits

turns.content: I've been reading about how custom traits can be derived using proc macros, but I still don't fully grasp what happens under the hood when I write #[derive(MyTrait)] on a struct. Can someone explain this to me?

When you use #[derive], Rust doesn't just generate code automatically like it does for standard library traits such as Debug or Clone. Instead, your compiler calls a proc macro that you've implemented in separate code, and that macro inspects the source file to determine what fields the struct has before generating an implementation of your trait. The generated code is essentially a manual implementation of MyTrait where the body contains logic specific to each field defined in the struct.

So I have to write a proc macro myself? That sounds tedious compared to just writing the impl block manually.

That makes sense for speed, but what if I want to combine multiple derives? For example, can I derive both MyTrait and another custom trait in one go?

I'm worried about the complexity though. Is there a way to avoid writing my own proc macro if I just want simple field access?

What about performance implications? Does deriving a custom trait impact runtime speed compared to manually impl'd code?

Okay, so I understand the flow now. One last thing - what if my trait requires implementing methods that depend on other traits? Can derive macros handle that dependency chain?

topic: Traits - Derive Macros for Custom Traits

turns.content: I'm trying to add a custom trait called `Loggable` to my data structures and I want to use derive, but the documentation just shows me the generated code without explaining how the macro actually works under the hood.

The confusion is common because official docs often skip the implementation details of proc macros. When you write `#[derive(Loggable)]`, the compiler runs your custom derive macro on the type, which generates the trait implementation code for you at compile time.

I see that it works, but I don't understand what happens when I have multiple derives in a single struct definition. If I add `#[derive(PartialEq, Loggable)]`, does the macro for Loggable just run once and ignore the other traits?

No, the derive macro runs separately for each trait in your list. When you combine derives like `#[derive(PartialEq, Loggable)]`, the compiler generates distinct code blocks for every trait specified.

Wait, I'm getting confused because when I try to add a new field to my struct and re-derive, it seems like the macro doesn't automatically update if I haven't updated the macro definition itself. Is there some kind of caching or something?
That's not caching; that's how Rust derives work. The derive macro only processes the struct once at compile time, so you must update your macro code when you change the target type.
I'm getting frustrated because I keep breaking my build just trying to add logging fields without updating the macro. Can't there be a way to make it dynamic or something?
The derive approach is static by design, but you can achieve dynamic behavior by using a procedural macro that inspects the struct's field names at compile time and generates code accordingly.
So if I want to add fields without changing my macro logic every time, do I need to write a custom proc macro that parses the struct definition itself?
Exactly. You'd write a procedural macro in Rust that receives the source code of your struct as input and generates the appropriate implementation for Loggable based on what fields it finds.

topic: Traits - Derive Macros for Custom Traits

turns.content: I'm coming back to Rust after five years and I thought I could just use #[derive(MyTrait)] on any struct like I did with Clone, but it seems my custom trait isn't being applied automatically.

You're right that #[derive] works for built-in traits like Debug or PartialEq, but a custom trait requires an implementation. You cannot just derive it unless you've written a proc macro to generate the body of your trait automatically.
Oh okay, so I have to write a whole macro crate? That sounds like too much boilerplate for something simple. What exactly does the generated code look like?

Exactly, that is why we use proc macros instead of manual implementation. A typical implementation of #[derive(MyTrait)] via a proc macro would generate an impl MyTrait for your struct T by calling methods on the fields and implementing the trait's logic based on their types.
Wait, so if I have multiple derives in my code like #[derive(Debug, Serialize)] they all run together? Or does one block out the others?

They run sequentially and compose without conflict because each derive targets a specific trait implementation. You can stack derives as long as none of them try to implement the same underlying logic for conflicting purposes.
That makes sense, but what if my custom MyTrait depends on another crate's types? Will that break the macro expansion?

If your trait logic relies on external crates like serde_json or tokio, you must ensure those dependencies are available at compile time. The proc macro will only generate correct code if it can resolve all the types it needs from the crate graph.
So I shouldn't rely on #[derive] for complex logic? Just keep it simple like Clone?

Not necessarily, but you should avoid using #[derive] for traits that require deep understanding of the crate's internal structure. For most cases, a well-designed proc macro can handle complex logic while still being usable with #[derive].

topic: Traits - Derive Macros for Custom Traits

turns.content: I'm trying to figure out how this new trait system I'm seeing in Rust connects back to my old C++ experience where everything was all about virtual functions and inheritance hierarchies, but it feels different here. The docs mention something like #[derive(MyTrait)] which sounds like a macro trick rather than manual implementation.

That comparison is spot on because both languages solve the problem of adding behavior to data structures without cluttering them with repetitive code. In Rust you use proc macros to generate the boilerplate automatically, so when you write #[derive(MyTrait)] it's actually running a macro that expands into a full implementation block for your type. You can think of this as Rust's way of handling virtual method tables but through compile-time expansion instead of runtime dispatch.
So if I just add the derive attribute to my struct, does the compiler magically write out all the logic for me? Is there any manual work involved at all?

The compiler does generate the heavy lifting automatically, but you still have to define what MyTrait actually looks like in a separate module. That definition is where you specify which fields of your struct need to be exposed or how they should interact with other types. Once that trait definition exists, the derive macro will expand into an impl block for every type that uses #[derive(MyTrait)], ensuring that all instances satisfy the contract you defined.
Okay so I'm building a custom trait called MyTrait and then applying it to structs in my crate. But what if I want multiple traits on one struct? Can I just stack them like #[derive(First, Second)]?

Yes absolutely, you can chain derive attributes together because the compiler treats each macro invocation as an independent expansion step. When your struct has #[derive(First, Second)] it will run First's proc macro to generate its impl block, then immediately run Second's proc macro to generate another one without any conflicts. This is actually more flexible than C++ where you'd have to manually implement multiple interfaces or use traits with the same name.
I'm wondering about the performance implications too since I thought macros might add some overhead at runtime like in C++. Does #[derive(MyTrait)] really eliminate that concern?

You're right to question that because macros are purely compile-time operations, so there is zero runtime cost associated with them. The macro runs when the compiler processes your code and produces optimized Rust code afterwards; it never executes at runtime like a virtual function call or dynamic binding would. This means your #[derive(MyTrait)] structs behave exactly like regular types without any hidden overhead.
Wait so if I were to write out the impl block manually instead of using derive, what would that look like for a simple struct with some fields?

If you wrote it manually instead of relying on #[derive(MyTrait)] you'd need to explicitly implement MyTrait and then list every field from your struct in the trait body. For example you might write impl MyTrait for YourStruct { fn foo(&self) -> bool { self.field_a && !self.field_b } }, but with derive the macro handles that expansion so you don't have to manually enumerate those fields.
That makes sense, so I can use this pattern to automate common boilerplate across my entire codebase without touching every single file? It's basically like a meta-programming tool?

Exactly it functions as a powerful automation layer because the derive macro scans your types and generates consistent implementations everywhere. You define the behavior once in MyTrait and then apply it to dozens of structs with just one attribute per type, which is much cleaner than writing duplicate impl blocks manually.
Hmm but I'm still worried about making mistakes if I get the trait definition wrong since there's no runtime check like in some languages. Is there a way to debug what the macro actually generates?

You can absolutely inspect what the macro produces by using #![feature(expand)] or similar compiler flags depending on your Rust version, which will show you exactly what code the derive macro expanded into before compilation. This lets you verify that MyTrait's implementation matches your expectations and helps catch errors early in development rather than waiting until runtime.

topic: Traits - Derive Macros for Custom Traits

turns.content: I just finished implementing a custom trait called MyTrait for my data structures, and I wrote a proc-macro to generate the boilerplate code. It works perfectly for my side project this weekend, but I'm wondering if there's an idiomatic way to handle this instead of manually maintaining the generated logic.

That is a very smart approach for your situation since you want to avoid repetitive code. The Rust ecosystem has matured significantly around derive macros, so relying on a standard crate like `derive_more` or building your own proc-macro is usually the preferred path over manual implementation. When people ask about deriving traits manually, they often miss the fact that modern Rust encourages using specialized crates for common patterns.

I've been thinking about it more and I realized my current solution has a flaw where if I add a new field to my struct, I have to update the macro logic manually. Is there a way to make this completely automatic so the compiler generates everything for me?

You are absolutely right to be concerned about manual updates; that is exactly why Rust's derive system exists. When you use `#[derive]` on a custom trait, the macro expands at compile time based on your struct definition, which means adding fields requires no code changes in your logic layer. The compiler will automatically generate the implementation for every field you declare.
That sounds great, but I'm still confused about how to actually define a custom trait that can be derived without writing hundreds of lines of macro code myself. Can you show me the exact steps?

Defining a custom trait is straightforward; you simply declare it with `trait MyTrait` and then use the derive attribute on implementations like your struct. The key insight is that you do not need to write the expansion logic manually anymore, so you only define the trait once, and the compiler handles the rest.
I see now, but what if I want to compose multiple traits together? Like deriving both MyTrait and another custom one at the same time?

Composing derives is a common pattern in advanced Rust usage. You can stack multiple derive attributes on a single struct, which allows your implementation to satisfy several traits simultaneously without extra work. This is particularly useful when you want to bundle related functionality into one type.
I'm trying to understand the difference between using `impl Trait` directly versus using a derive macro for this specific case. Which approach feels more idiomatic in production code?

Using a derive macro is generally more idiomatic when dealing with complex trait implementations because it offloads the boilerplate to the compiler. Direct `impl Trait` blocks are better suited for simple cases or when you need fine-grained control over the generated code, but for your weekend project, the derive approach will save significant time.

You mentioned offloading boilerplate, but I'm still worried about performance implications. Does using a proc-macro to generate this trait implementation impact runtime speed compared to hand-written code?

There is no runtime penalty for using derive macros because the expansion happens entirely at compile time, so your actual execution path remains identical whether you use a macro or manual implementation. The only difference is compilation time.
That makes sense, but I want to make sure I understand how to actually write the trait definition itself. Can you give me an example of what `trait MyTrait` should look like?

Your custom trait definition looks standard; it declares the methods your types must implement, and then you apply `#[derive(MyTrait)]` to structs that need this behavior. The macro will expand into an `impl MyTrait for YourStruct` block automatically.
I think I finally get it now. One last question: if I later decide to add a method that requires manual logic, can I still use derive macros?

Yes, you can mix and match; the macro will only generate code for fields or methods it handles automatically, while you manually implement any custom logic in an explicit `impl MyTrait` block. This hybrid approach is common when your requirements evolve beyond simple field access.
